serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 300 g (one bowl), comprising the spiced broth with mutton marrow bones.

nutrient_source.title: Mutton (Goat) Bone Soup / Rasam — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, adapted for bone broth preparation
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nalli Elumbhu Rasam is a South Indian spiced mutton marrow-bone broth. No exact entry exists in ICMR-NIN for this dish. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN data for mutton bone soup / rasam preparations (lean mutton with bone, cooked), cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entry for 'Lamb, domestic, composite of trimmed retail cuts, cooked' (FDC ID 174381) and published compositional data for South Indian rasam broths. The dish is predominantly a thin, spiced broth with dissolved marrow and collagen; macronutrient profile reflects a dilute meat broth rather than a solid meat dish.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Meat-Based Broths and Soups — analogous estimate based on low-carbohydrate meat broth category
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Nalli Elumbhu Rasam. GI estimated at 30 based on the University of Sydney GI Database category for low-carbohydrate meat soups and broths. The dish contains approximately 1.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g, predominantly from tamarind and tomato; the glycemic response is minimal. GL per 300 g serving = (30 × 4.5 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 1.35, rounded to 1.4.

hypertension.guidance: Nalli Elumbhu Rasam contains approximately 280 mg sodium per 100 g (840 mg per 300 g serving), which represents a significant portion of the 1500–2000 mg daily sodium limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; portion size should be limited and no additional salt added.

type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 1.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g and a low estimated glycemic index of 30, Nalli Elumbhu Rasam has a negligible glycemic load and is generally suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in standard portions, though sodium intake should still be monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat and sodium from bone marrow
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The marrow bones contribute saturated fat (approximately 0.7 g per 100 g) and the broth is moderately high in sodium; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit serving frequency and consider requesting reduced-salt preparation to keep saturated fat and sodium within cardioprotective dietary targets.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Nalli Elumbhu Rasam is a traditional South Indian spiced mutton marrow-bone broth, originating from Tamil Nadu and widely consumed across South India and among the South Indian diaspora in Singapore and Malaysia. The dish is prepared by slow-simmering mutton nalli (marrow bones) with tamarind water, tomatoes, black pepper, cumin, garlic, curry leaves, dried red chillies, turmeric, and coriander seeds until the broth is deeply flavoured and the marrow has dissolved into the liquid. The result is a thin, intensely spiced, tangy, and peppery broth with a rich umami depth from the bone marrow and collagen. Texturally it is a clear-to-lightly-cloudy liquid with soft bone pieces. Rasam in South Indian culinary tradition refers to a thin, sour, and peppery broth typically consumed as a digestive, as a soup course, or mixed with steamed rice. The nalli (marrow bone) variant is a more substantial, protein-rich version. Nutritionally, the dish is low in carbohydrates (approximately 1.5 g per 100 g), moderate in protein (approximately 4.2 g per 100 g), and relatively low in fat (approximately 1.8 g per 100 g), making it a low-calorie, low-glycaemic-index food. Sodium content is moderate to high due to salt and tamarind. It is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free. Clinically, it is suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its negligible glycaemic load, but sodium content warrants attention for those with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is commonly served as a starter or side broth in South Indian meals and is also valued in traditional medicine as a restorative bone broth.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Nalli Elumbhu Rasam exists in ICMR-NIN or any other approved primary source. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN mutton bone soup data and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central cooked lamb/mutton entries. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database low-carbohydrate meat broth category. Confidence is medium rather than low because the dish composition is well-characterised and the adaptation basis is defensible.
notes: Nutrient values are adapted estimates; no exact compositional analysis for Nalli Elumbhu Rasam was found in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central. Values reflect a dilute spiced mutton marrow-bone broth at approximately 300 g serving size. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source data for this preparation. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source data for this preparation. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source data for this preparation. Trans fat is reported as 0.0; this is confirmed negligible for a home-style or restaurant-style South Indian broth preparation with no hydrogenated fats. GI is an analogous estimate set at 30 (low category, ≤55) based on the University of Sydney GI Database low-carbohydrate meat broth category; no direct GI measurement for this dish was found. GL per 300 g serving calculated as (30 × 4.5 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 1.4. Halal status is assumed based on the dish being a mutton preparation served at an Indian Muslim-style restaurant context; verification with the specific restaurant is recommended. The dish is listed under field survey data from Manam - Upper Circular Road.
